name: Dopamine Signaling Pathway
summary: Dopamine signaling regulates reward, motivation, movement, cognition, and psychosis. Dopamine is synthesized in substantia nigra and ventral tegmental area; released into striatum and prefrontal cortex. DRD2 is the primary postsynaptic receptor in mesolimbic and nigrostriatal pathways; D2 antagonists are antipsychotics. Dopamine is metabolized by COMT and MAO. Hyperdopaminergia in striatum is implicated in schizophrenia; hypodopaminergia in addiction. DRD2 variants influence receptor density, antipsychotic response, and addiction vulnerability. Psychosocial stress and substance use (alcohol, tobacco) modulate dopamine signaling and interact with genetic susceptibility.

environmental_triggers.trigger_type: Chronic stress alters dopaminergic tone and prefrontal-striatal balance
Alcohol acutely increases dopamine; chronic use causes neuroadaptation

key_genes.role_in_pathway: D2 dopamine receptor; primary target of antipsychotics; variants affect receptor function and addiction risk
Serotonin transporter; modulates dopamine-serotonin interaction; relevant to comorbid depression and addiction

tissue_specificity.expression_evidence: Primary site of DRD2; reward and movement

linked_diseases.pathway_role: Hyperdopaminergia hypothesis; DRD2 is antipsychotic target

linked_exposures.pathway_effect: Alcohol dependence involves dopamine dysregulation; DRD2 variants
Stress precipitates psychosis and depression; gene-environment interaction
